name: Sauron
description: Elm-inspired, minimal virtual-DOM library with SSR support

description.value: A versatile web framework and library for building client-side and/or server-side web applications, following The Elm Architecture with a strong focus on ergonomics, simplicity and elegance
description.comment: Combines the repo/crate tagline with the README's Elm-architecture framing (model/view/update).

license.comment: MIT only (SPDX 'MIT' per the GitHub license field and crates.io metadata), not the MIT OR Apache-2.0 dual-license common to most Rust web frameworks.

crates-io.comment: sauron is the top-level crate; the workspace also publishes sauron-core, sauron-node-macro, and the standalone VDOM crate mt-dom.

documentation.comment: Primary docs are the crate-level API docs on docs.rs; there is no separate guide/book, the README and the 31 in-repo examples serve as the narrative documentation.

maintenance-status.comment: Last release (0.70.0) and last commit both 2025-01-27, i.e. ~18 months before the 2026-07-19 check with no activity since; not archived. Beyond the 6-month 'actively-maintained' window and trending dormant, but the long-running solo project is not declared abandoned.

maturity.comment: Long-lived (since 2019) but still pre-1.0 (0.70.0) with a churning API across 0.6x releases and no widely-documented production usage; classified experimental for consistency with peer pre-1.0 entrants.

corporate-foundation-backing.comment: No corporate/foundation sponsor; effectively a solo project by ivanceras (Jovansonlee Cesar), who accounts for the overwhelming majority of commits.

reactivity-model.comment: State propagates via The Elm Architecture (model/view/update message loop) — Sauron is the canonical Rust 'elm' example per RESEARCH.md; the render path itself builds a virtual DOM and diff/patches it (dom module 'provides diffing algorithm which returns patches').

component-model.comment: Apps/components implement the Application/Component traits (view/update/stylesheet) on a struct; also supports web-components/custom-elements built from the same trait implementations.

view-authoring.comment: Views can be written with the node! html-style macro or with function/builder syntax (div(), etc.); the examples ship both '-macro-syntax' and builder variants (e.g. simple_builder.rs, minimal vs minimal-macro-syntax).

fine-grained-updates.comment: Updates go through virtual-DOM diff/patch, not fine-grained signal-targeted node updates.

uses-virtual-dom.comment: Maintains a virtual DOM (vdom module) with a diff() that returns the patches needed to transform old_node into new_node; supports keyed diffing via key().

async-await-in-components.comment: Async data loading is handled the Elm way — via Cmd/Effects that dispatch a Msg on completion (see fetch-data examples) — rather than awaiting inside components/resources suspense-style.

global-state-solution.comment: The single Application model is the app-wide state per the Elm architecture; there is no dedicated context-injection or signal-store primitive for cross-component global state.

compiles-to.comment: Client compiles to WebAssembly via wasm-bindgen; the same view code renders to a String on the native server side for SSR.

primary-build-tool.comment: README recommends wasm-pack (build --target web --release) plus basic-http-server; no first-party build tool. A csr-tailwind-trunk example shows Trunk works too, but wasm-pack is the documented default.

dev-server-hmr.comment: Documented flow is a wasm-pack build served by basic-http-server; no first-party hot-reload or live-reload (the Trunk example provides live-reload via the third-party Trunk tool).

minimal-wasm-bundle-size.comment: No reliably documented, methodically-measured hello-world gzipped .wasm size available; per RESEARCH.md, prefer null over an unmethodical number.

js-interop.comment: Built on wasm-bindgen with web-sys/js-sys for browser/JS API access; also re-exports serde-wasm-bindgen for serializing state across the JS boundary.

js-interop-ergonomics.comment: Standard wasm-bindgen/web-sys path with the usual boilerplate; no dedicated JS-interop conveniences beyond the underlying crates.

no-std-minimal-runtime.comment: No no_std mode; relies on std and the wasm-bindgen runtime.

client-side-rendering-spa.comment: Program::mount_to_body(App::new()) boots a WASM client that renders and diff/patches the DOM in the browser (baseline SPA mode).

server-side-rendering.comment: SSR is a first-class, advertised capability: views render to an HTML String on the server (progressive-rendering and ssr-forms examples).

static-site-generation.comment: Static site generation is explicitly listed as a supported capability (render views to static HTML at build time).

hydration.comment: No true DOM rehydration: the progressive-rendering approach serializes the server App state to JSON, recreates the App on the client, and replaces the <main> element with a fresh client render rather than reattaching to and rehydrating the server-rendered DOM tree.

server-functions.comment: No integrated, isomorphic server-function mechanism; client/server communication is written manually (e.g. fetch in the fetch-data examples).

streaming-ssr.comment: Although Sauron lists 'progressive rendering' as a feature and the example describes streaming portions of a page, the implementation renders a complete HTML document served via warp rather than confirmed out-of-order/in-order chunked streaming.

backend-integration.comment: SSR produces a plain String usable with any server; there is no framework-specific integration crate. The progressive-rendering example happens to use warp.

isomorphic-routing.comment: Sauron ships no router at all, so there is no shared server/client routing.

official-router.comment: No first-party routing module in the public API; routing is left to the application or third-party crates.

nested-routes.comment: No official router, hence no nested-route support.

dynamic-route-params.comment: No official router, hence no parameterized routes out of the box.

typed-routes.comment: No official router, hence no type-checked routes.

route-guards.comment: No official router, hence no navigation guards.

lazy-code-split-routes.comment: No first-party lazy/code-split route loading (no router).

styling-approach.comment: First-class CSS-in-Rust via the jss!/jss_with_media! macros (stylesheet() method) and inline styles via the style! macro; plain/external CSS also works. A csr-tailwind-trunk example shows Tailwind is usable too.

scoped-styles.comment: jss! generates a global stylesheet injected via the Application's stylesheet()/style() hooks; styles are not automatically component-scoped.

component-library-ecosystem.comment: Essentially no ready-made third-party UI component-library crates; users build their own components.

forms-inputs.comment: Inputs are wired manually via events (on_input etc.) that dispatch messages reading/writing the model; no two-way-binding directive or dedicated form-helper library (ssr-forms example).

svg-canvas-support.comment: Dedicated svg module/macros for authoring SVG in views (svg-clock, svg-graph examples); canvas is reachable via web-sys.

suspense-loading-ui.comment: No declarative suspense/loading-boundary primitive; loading states are modeled explicitly in the app state.

documentation-quality.comment: API docs on docs.rs plus a substantial README, but no cohesive guide/book or tutorial series; narrative docs are thin and lean heavily on the (many) examples.

api-ergonomics.comment: The Elm model and node! macro are clean and the project explicitly targets ergonomics/simplicity, but the struct+trait wiring and manual message plumbing add some boilerplate.

compile-times.comment: Macro-based node! views; comparable to peer macro-driven Rust web frameworks for a small app.

examples-count.comment: ~31 example projects in the examples/ directory (counter, todomvc, data-viewer, svg-clock, fetch-data, progressive-rendering, ssr-forms, custom-element, etc.), well into the >20 'extensive' band.

interactive-playground.comment: No online playground/REPL or hosted example gallery; the author maintains standalone demo apps (e.g. the ultron editor) but there is no unified interactive playground.

browser-devtools.comment: No dedicated Sauron devtools/inspector beyond generic browser tools.

testing-support.comment: Uses wasm-bindgen-test for browser-based tests (dev-dependency); no dedicated component-testing harness beyond that.

github-stars.comment: 2,065 stars per the GitHub API; checked 2026-07-19.

crates-io-downloads.comment: All-time downloads of the sauron crate; checked 2026-07-19.

contributors.comment: ~11 contributors per the GitHub contributors API; checked 2026-07-19. Development is overwhelmingly dominated by ivanceras (~2,263 of the commits).

community-size.comment: Effectively a single-maintainer project (ivanceras) with a handful of one-off contributors and no advertised dedicated community chat.

ecosystem-crates.comment: In-tree crates (sauron-core, sauron-node-macro, mt-dom) cover the core; essentially no third-party routers, component kits, or integrations.

notable-production-users.comment: No widely-documented external production users; the notable apps built with it (e.g. the author's ultron text editor and a HackerNews clone) are the maintainer's own projects/demos.

meta-framework-full-stack-kit.comment: No separate meta-framework/full-stack kit; SSR, SSG, and progressive rendering are built into the core sauron crate rather than a dedicated fullstack layer.
